content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

BAGATELOS & FADEM
THE INTERNATIONAL BUILDING
601 CALIFORNIA STREET
SUITE 1801
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108

BARRY FADEM
PETER A. BAGATELOS
WES VAN WINKLE

TELEPHONE
(415) 982-7100
FAX
(415) 982-1085

December 28, 1989

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca. 95804-0807

Dear Scott:

     This will confirm our telephone conversation
today regarding whether a ballot measure committee
controlled by an officeholder may accept contributions
from other candidate-controlled committees. I had
initially spoken with Wayne Imberi about this subject
also.

     I told you that a client had previously called
the technical assistance office for advice and was
told that such contributions could be made. In light
of all of the confusing changes in advice and direction
that have been caused by the Proposition 68/73 situation,
I felt that I should re-check that advice today.

     When I spoke with Wayne, he confirmed that the
technical assistance office has been giving advice
consistently this year that such contributions could
be accepted by controlled ballot measure committees.
However, he said that recently you had taken a contrary
view in a letter to Mr. Hitaick. Apparently, Wayne
followed this lead in a letter of advice that he also
recently sent to Cary Davidson.

     My client's difficulty is that the committee relied
on advice and guidance previously provided by the FPPC
and accepted such contributions during this year. The
committee has continuing obligations and a deficit at
the current time. It solicited contributions and relied
to its detriment in accepting those contributions and in
incurring financial obligations. The committee may not
have the resources or ability to refund those contributions.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
December 28, 1989
page two


You indicated that, while there has been no written
advice issued until just recently, the technical assistance office has been giving advice that such contributions
could be made. I asked for your thoughts as to what
action should now be taken. You said that the committee
should try to undo the contributions through refunds, if
the committee could do so. You acknowledged that this
could be difficult if the committee has already spent
the funds.

This is a very untenable situation. My client in
good faith believed that it was acting consistent with
current law interpretations when it accepted such
contributions, after actively seeking technical assistance.
My client had no reason to assume that it could not rely
on such advice. The advice given seemed, at the time
(which was around June, 1989), to be fairly routine and
not of a complexity that would have suggested requesting a
written opinion. Such "wind-shifting" in advice would
suggest that any reliance by parties prior to the current
advice, and based on prior advice to the contrary, should
be subject to a grace period of acceptability. In other
words, prior transactions should be deemed not illegal.

Thank you for your advice and comments.

Sincerely,

Peter A. Bagatelos
Peter A. Bagatelos

sections.question_synthetic: The attorney seeks guidance on whether a ballot measure committee controlled by an officeholder may accept contributions from other candidate-controlled committees, given conflicting FPPC technical assistance advice and recent contrary letters of advice.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming letter from an attorney to FPPC staff, not an FPPC response/advice letter. No formal question or conclusion sections are present. The letter describes a factual scenario and requests guidance on how to remedy a situation where conflicting FPPC advice was given. Document discusses Proposition 68/73 context and requests consideration of a grace period for prior transactions based on prior advice.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The attorney seeks guidance on whether a ballot measure committee controlled by an officeholder may accept contributions from other candidate-controlled committees, given conflicting FPPC technical assistance advice and recent contrary letters of advice.

embedding.summary: Attorney Peter Bagatelos writes to Scott Hallabrin at the FPPC regarding conflicting guidance on contributions to controlled ballot measure committees. The client committee previously relied on FPPC technical assistance office advice that such contributions were permissible, but has since learned of contrary positions taken in recent FPPC letters, creating uncertainty about the legality of contributions already accepted.
